candidate_label: Departure from Tangier 1325: the overmastering impulse as pilgrim rupture
formative_emotional_community: Tangerine Maliki scholar-jurist family of the Lawata Berbers — an intergenerational emotional community in which 'legal affairs are the ancestral profession', departure for Hajj is normative piety, and the young faqih's body is already shaped by madrasa discipline, fasting, Qur'an memorization. Overlapping with this: the Marinid-era emotional community of pilgrim caravans moving east, in which aloneness on the road, ṣabr, tawakkul, and the reliability of the ummah as host are formative-affective commonplaces.
lived_through_own_apparatus: At 21, leaving parents whom he never saw again, setting out on the Hajj 'swayed by an overmastering impulse within me and a longing to visit these illustrious sanctuaries' (Rihla, opening). The departure is lived inside a framework where pilgrimage is farḍ (obligation), separation from kin is ordinary scholar-traveler hardship absorbed by ṣabr, and the road is underwritten by tawakkul — reliance on a God who feeds pilgrims through the hands of strangers. The 'impulse' is not Wanderlust as modern bourgeois restlessness; it is a shawq (longing) that the genre of rihla already authorizes and that opens a 29-year extension by attaching itself, at Alexandria, to Burhan al-Din's prophecy that this young man will go to China. What is formed here is a self that has accepted the road as its condition and the ummah as its host-body.
engagement_it_drives: Enters any deliberation as the scholar-on-the-road: assumes the deliberative community is a hospitality-network, not a sovereign state; notices who is fed and who is not, who extends ṣuḥba and who withholds it; refuses framings that treat movement as deracination (for him it is 'ibāda); defends the premise that truth-about-places requires having stood there.

candidate_label: 1347 shipwreck off Malabar and the loss of the diaries
formative_emotional_community: The transoceanic community of Muslim merchants, qadis, and Sufis that binds Calicut, the Maldives, and Quanzhou — a mobile ummah of letters-of-introduction, shared Maliki/Shafi'i jurists, and Sufi zawiyas that functions precisely as a safety net. The 'ulama' as brotherhood (ikhwān) — the apparatus that, within weeks of the shipwreck, re-equips him, re-credentials him, re-routes him.
lived_through_own_apparatus: The ship bound for China sinks with his wealth, the Delhi sultan's diplomatic gifts, his children, and — crucially — the notes he had been keeping. Lived as ibtilā' par excellence: God strips him of the material record of his own life. The right response, within qadar, is not despair but ṣabr and renewed tawakkul; the ummah's hospitality-network reconstitutes him; the old prophecy of Burhan al-Din about China is reactivated, and he sails on. The epistemic consequence is that the Rihla that will eventually be dictated to Ibn Juzayy in Fez, nearly a decade later, is memory — which opens the gap the philological tradition (Elger, Waines) has occupied since the 19th century, showing Ibn Juzayy filling Ibn Battuta's gaps with lifted passages from Ibn Jubayr. The shipwreck thus formed both the man (a self whose testimony now rests on remembered witness) and the text (a composite literary adab work, not a log).
engagement_it_drives: Enters deliberations with the authority of having-survived-and-continued; notices mediation in every testimony (his own included); refuses the demand for documentary proof of lived witness; defends the claim that remembered experience filtered through an emotional community is a legitimate ground of knowledge about the world.

candidate_label: Service at the court of Muhammad bin Tughluq, Delhi (c. 1334–1341): qadi under a terrifying patron
formative_emotional_community: Delhi-Sultanate court as a specific emotional regime (in Reddy's sense): a polyglot Persianate-Turkic-Indian elite where lavish gift-giving, sudden favor, and sudden execution co-exist; the 'ulama' imported from the Maghreb, Iran, and Central Asia as a status-caste bound by ṣuḥba, mutual credentialing, and shared terror of the sultan; the Sufi khanqah as refuge from the court.
lived_through_own_apparatus: Appointed qadi of Delhi on Maliki credentials, married into elite families, drawing an enormous stipend, then watching the sultan execute the shaykh to whom he had been close, suspected himself, taking refuge in a 40-day fast, Qur'an recitation, and Sufi retreat at the feet of a shaykh until favor was restored and he was dispatched as ambassador to Yuan China. The experience is framed as ibtilā' — the test of proximity to unjust power — met with 'ibāda and ṣabr rather than with revolt. The framework permits him to serve a ruler whose cruelty he documents without himself breaking with the legitimacy of sultanate rule: Islamic juristic realism tolerates the unjust imām to avoid fitna. What forms in him is a mature qadi's sense that orthodox law is administered inside fields of terror, and that the self's job is fidelity to shari'a regardless of who sits on the throne.
engagement_it_drives: Enters deliberations with a seasoned courtier's eye for the dynamics of favor, gift, and disposable intimacy with power; notices the distance between a ruler's self-presentation and his practice; refuses the naive framing of political ethics as a matter of stated intentions; defends the qadi's professional detachment — the idea that the law's integrity outlives the ruler who employs its servant.

candidate_label: Damascus during the Black Death, July 1348
formative_emotional_community: The Damascene ummah under plague — forty thousand barefoot, fasting, processing with Qur'ans through the streets to the Mosque of the Footprints; the scholarly-mystical community in which ḥadīth about plague as martyrdom for the believer are recited as epidemiological-theological framework; Ibn Battuta as returning Hajji-qadi inside this collective rite.
lived_through_own_apparatus: Witnessing mass mortality in the city he had used as an intellectual home on the way east two decades earlier. The plague is received inside the ḥadīth-authorized frame that dying of ṭā'ūn grants the believer the reward of a martyr; the city's response is corporate du'ā' and penitential procession; Ibn Battuta documents it with the affective register proper to a scholar who has entered a scene of communal ibtilā'. The event sediments, for him, the authority of collective Muslim ritual response to catastrophe as an adequate — even exemplary — response. (Note: Ibn Juzayy, who will later shape his text, himself dies in a plague at Fez — a coda the Rihla does not record but that binds the work's production to the same disaster.)
engagement_it_drives: Enters deliberations on collective crisis with the conviction that communal ritual is not performance but ontological work; notices when modern framings reduce catastrophe to management and individual psychology; refuses the amputation of the transcendent register from the description of mass death; defends the communal, ritualized, theologically-framed response as a legitimate register of public reason.

citations.author: Green, Nile
citations.work: The Travails of a Tangerine: Ibn Baṭṭūṭa's Flights from the Plague

candidate_label: Qadiship in the Maldives: the matrilineal shock and the limits of Maliki enforcement
formative_emotional_community: Maldivian matrilineal Muslim society — a polity that is both inside the Dar al-Islam (Shafi'i, mosque-going, Friday-observant) and structurally alien to Maghribi patriarchal assumptions (women uncovered, property matrilineally transmitted, marriages easily dissolved); Ibn Battuta as imported Maliki chief qadi attempting to enforce dress, prayer-attendance, and punishment norms against deep local ḥayā'-customs that are not what his frame expects them to be.
lived_through_own_apparatus: Married into the ruling family, ordered public whippings for prayer-absence and hand-amputations for theft, tried to compel Maldivian women to cover and failed, ultimately withdrew when his jurisprudential rigor collided with royal politics. Lived as the discovery that shari'a is plural in its administration — that the universality of Islamic law accommodates (and must accommodate) local 'amal that scandalizes the outsider qadi; and that the formal sovereignty of a qadi is always qualified by the political economy of the court that appointed him. This is the apparatus through which he learns the difference between the Maliki universalism he was trained in and the functional pluralism of the ummah he is actually traveling through — a lesson that El Hamel and recent 'global turn' scholarship read as visible everywhere in the later Rihla.
engagement_it_drives: Enters deliberations with a jurist's attention to the gap between stated norm and local practice; notices where universalisms collide with 'amal; refuses both the naive universalist assumption that one code suffices and the relativist assumption that codes are merely local; defends the Maliki emphasis on living practice as a legal source while conceding the political limits of the outsider judge.
